name: macd
whatItMeasures: The MACD measures the relationship between two exponential moving averages to identify changes in trend momentum, direction, and strength. It consists of the MACD line (difference between 12-day and 26-day EMA), the signal line (9-day EMA of the MACD line), and the histogram (difference between the two). Developed by Gerald Appel in the late 1970s.

parameters.name: Fast EMA
parameters.rationale: The 12-day EMA captures short-term price momentum. This is the original value specified by Appel and remains the universal standard.

parameters.name: Slow EMA
parameters.rationale: The 26-day EMA represents the intermediate-term trend baseline. The 12/26 combination has been validated across decades of market data.

parameters.name: Signal Line
parameters.rationale: The 9-day EMA of the MACD line smooths out noise and provides a trigger line for crossover signals.

howToRead: When the MACD line crosses above the signal line, it is a bullish signal (buying momentum is increasing).
When the MACD line crosses below the signal line, it is a bearish signal (selling momentum is increasing).
The histogram shows the distance between the MACD and signal lines — growing bars mean accelerating momentum.
When both MACD and signal line are above zero, the overall trend is bullish; below zero, bearish.
Divergence between MACD and price action (price makes new high but MACD does not) can foreshadow reversals.

signalRules.buy: MACD line crosses above the signal line (bullish crossover), especially when occurring below the zero line.
signalRules.sell: MACD line crosses below the signal line (bearish crossover), especially when occurring above the zero line.
signalRules.neutral: MACD and signal line are intertwined near the zero line with no clear crossover.

limitations: MACD is a lagging indicator — crossovers occur after the move has already begun.
In sideways or choppy markets, MACD generates frequent false crossover signals.
MACD does not indicate overbought/oversold conditions — it has no bounded range.
The default parameters may not suit all timeframes or asset classes; shorter-term traders sometimes adjust them.
MACD signals should be confirmed with other indicators (RSI, volume, trend filters) for higher reliability.
evidence: MACD is one of the most widely validated technical indicators in financial literature. The signal-line crossover strategy has been extensively backtested across multiple markets and timeframes. While standalone MACD trading produces mixed results in range-bound markets, it is consistently effective as a trend confirmation tool. Its strength is in identifying the direction and acceleration of trends rather than as a standalone entry/exit system.
